36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.

41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65

68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74

76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.

88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.

92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.

96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

128	## Must-fix-this-iter

131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.

162	- ...

167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.

If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.

79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations

They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. 102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.

112	/-! ## §1. From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover -/
113	
114	/-- **Trivialising affine cover** (Stacks 01CS / Definition 17.25.1).
115	A locally trivial module `M` admits an indexed trivialising affine cover: an
116	index type `I`, a family of affine opens `U i` covering `X`, and for each `i` a
117	`𝒪_{U_i}`-module isomorphism `M|_{U_i} ≅ 𝒪_{U_i}`.
118	
119	The witness repackages the pointwise existential `IsLocallyTrivial M` (take
120	`I := X` and assign each point its chosen neighbourhood). -/
121	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover {M : X.Modules}
122	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
123	    ∃ (I : Type u) (U : I → X.Opens),
124	      (∀ i, IsAffineOpen (U i)) ∧ iSup U = ⊤ ∧
125	        ∀ i, Nonempty (M.restrict (U i).ι ≅
126	          SheafOfModules.unit (U i : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) := by
127	  refine ⟨X, fun x => (hM x).choose, fun x => (hM x).choose_spec.2.1, ?_,
128	    fun x => (hM x).choose_spec.2.2⟩
129	  rw [eq_top_iff]
130	  intro x _
131	  rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_iSup]
132	  exact ⟨x, (hM x).choose_spec.1⟩
133	
134	/-! ## §1b. The canonical finite presentation of the unit module -/
135	
136	section UnitPresentation
137	
138	universe u₁ v₁
139	
140	variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
141	  {R : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}}
142	  [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{u}] [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat.{u}]
143	  [J.HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat.{u} AddCommGrpCat.{u})]
144	
145	/-- The canonical isomorphism `free PUnit ≅ unit R`: the free module on a single
146	generator is the unit module. -/
147	noncomputable def freeUnitIso :
148	    SheafOfModules.free.{u} (R := R) PUnit.{u + 1} ≅ SheafOfModules.unit R :=
149	  Limits.coproductUniqueIso (fun (_ : PUnit.{u + 1}) ↦ SheafOfModules.unit R)
150	
151	/-- The canonical generating sections of `unit R`: one generator (the image of
152	the free generator under `freeUnitIso`). -/
153	noncomputable def unitGenerators : (SheafOfModules.unit R).GeneratingSections where
154	  I := PUnit.{u + 1}
155	  s := (SheafOfModules.unit R).freeHomEquiv freeUnitIso.hom
156	  epi := by
157	    rw [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
158	    infer_instance
159	
160	/-- The canonical finite presentation of the unit module `unit R`: one generator,
161	no relations (its generating map is an isomorphism, so the relation kernel is the
162	zero object). -/
163	noncomputable def unitPresentation : (SheafOfModules.unit R).Presentation where
164	  generators := unitGenerators
165	  relations :=
166	    { I := PEmpty.{u + 1}
167	      s := PEmpty.elim
168	      epi := by
169	        have hiso : IsIso (unitGenerators (R := R)).π := by
170	          dsimp only [unitGenerators, SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.π]
171	          rw [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
172	          infer_instance
173	        have : Mono (unitGenerators (R := R)).π := inferInstance
174	        have hz : Limits.IsZero (Limits.kernel (unitGenerators (R := R)).π) :=
175	          Limits.isZero_kernel_of_mono _
176	        exact ⟨fun g h _ => hz.eq_of_src g h⟩ }
177	
178	instance : (unitPresentation (R := R)).IsFinite where
179	  isFiniteType_generators := ⟨inferInstanceAs (Finite PUnit.{u + 1})⟩
180	  isFiniteType_relations := ⟨inferInstanceAs (Finite PEmpty.{u + 1})⟩
181	
182	end UnitPresentation
183	
184	/-! ## §2. The trivial presentation on a chart -/
185	
186	/-- **The over↔restrict trivialisation bridge.** Transports the scheme-level
187	trivialisation `e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U` (an isomorphism in
188	`SheafOfModules (↑U).ringCatSheaf`, the category of modules on the *open
189	subscheme* `U`) to a slice-level trivialisation
190	`M.over U ≅ 𝒪_U` (in `SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`, the category of
191	modules on the *slice site* `J.over U`).
192	
193	The two source categories are equivalent — the open-immersion functor
194	`U.ι.opensFunctor : Opens ↥U ≌ {V ∈ Opens X | V ≤ U} = Over U` induces an
195	equivalence `SheafOfModules (↑U).ringCatSheaf ≌ SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`
196	identifying `M.restrict U.ι ↦ M.over U` and `unit ↦ unit` — but this
197	equivalence of *sheaf-of-modules* categories (the modules-level shadow of
198	`Opens.overEquivalence` / `overSliceSheafEquiv`) is not yet available in the
199	project, so the bridge is recorded here as a typed `sorry`. This is the single
200	missing ingredient for the whole `LineBundleCoherence` engine: everything else
201	(`chartPresentation`, `isFinitePresentation`, `isFiniteType`) is complete modulo
202	this isomorphism. -/
203	noncomputable def chartOverIso (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
204	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
205	    M.over U ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U) :=
206	  sorry
207	
208	/-- **Finite free presentation on a chart** (Stacks, "Modules of finite
209	presentation"). On a trivialising chart `U` — i.e. with an isomorphism
210	`M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U` — the restriction `M.over U` admits the trivial finite free
211	presentation (one generator, no relations), obtained by transporting the
212	canonical presentation of `SheafOfModules.unit` along the trivialisation
213	(through the `chartOverIso` bridge). -/
214	noncomputable def IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
215	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
216	    (M.over U).Presentation := by
217	  exact SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso (R := X.ringCatSheaf.over U)
218	    (M := SheafOfModules.unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)) (N := SheafOfModules.over M U)
219	    (chartOverIso M U e).inv (unitPresentation (R := X.ringCatSheaf.over U))
220	
221	instance (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
222	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
223	    (IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M U e).IsFinite := by
224	  dsimp only [IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation]
225	  infer_instance
226	
227	/-! ## §3. Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle -/
228	
229	/-- **Locally trivial `⟹` finitely presented** (Stacks 0B8M / Lemma 17.25.4).
230	A locally trivial module on a scheme is finitely presented: assemble a
231	`QuasicoherentData` from the trivialising affine cover
232	(`exists_trivializing_cover`), whose presentation at each index is the finite
233	free presentation of `chartPresentation`, and feed it to
234	`SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk`. No stalk computation or
235	neighbourhood-spreading is involved. -/
236	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation {M : X.Modules}
237	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
238	    M.IsFinitePresentation := by
239	  obtain ⟨I, U, hUaff, hUtop, hUiso⟩ := hM.exists_trivializing_cover
240	  -- assemble the quasi-coherent data, one finite chart presentation per index;
241	  -- `shrink` lands the index type in the site-object universe demanded by
242	  -- `IsFinitePresentation`'s existential.
243	  let q : M.QuasicoherentData :=
244	    { I := I
245	      X := U
246	      coversTop := by
247	        -- the trivialising affine opens cover the terminal object of the site
248	        intro W x hxW
249	        have hx : x ∈ iSup U := by rw [hUtop]; trivial
250	        rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_iSup] at hx
251	        obtain ⟨i, hi⟩ := hx
252	        exact ⟨U i ⊓ W, homOfLE inf_le_right, ⟨i, ⟨homOfLE inf_le_left⟩⟩, hi, hxW⟩
253	      presentation := fun i => IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M (U i) (hUiso i).some }
254	  have hsh : q.shrink.IsFinitePresentation := by
255	    apply SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation.mk
256	    intro i
257	    exact inferInstanceAs (IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M _ _).IsFinite
258	  exact { exists_quasicoherentData := ⟨q.shrink, hsh⟩ }
259	
260	/-- **Locally trivial `⟹` finite type and quasi-coherent** (corollary of
261	`isFinitePresentation`). Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
262	`SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation` and quasi-coherence by
263	`SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation`. -/
264	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType {M : X.Modules}
265	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
266	    M.IsFiniteType := by
267	  haveI := hM.isFinitePresentation
268	  infer_instance
269	
270	/-! ## §4. Rank one and flatness record -/
271	
272	/-- **Chart-local rank one and flatness** (Stacks, "Locally free sheaves":
273	finite locally free of rank `r`). On a trivialising chart the restriction
274	`M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U` is free of rank one over `𝒪_U`; in particular flat (`𝒪_U` is a flat
275	module over itself, and flatness is preserved by the isomorphism). The fact is
276	recorded chart-locally — as the rank-one free model `M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U = ⊕_{*} 𝒪_U`
277	the Quot embedding consumes — because Mathlib has no `SheafOfModules`-level
278	locally-free / flat / rank predicate. -/
279	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one {M : X.Modules}
280	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) (x : X) :
281	    ∃ U : X.Opens, x ∈ U ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
282	      Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) := by
283	  exact hM x
284	
285	end LineBundle

287	end Scheme

61	/-- **Section equivalence for the dual of the unit.** At an object `X`, endomorphisms of the
62	(restricted) unit `restr X 𝟙_ ⟶ restr X 𝟙_` are identified `R₀(X)`-linearly with `R₀(X)` itself,
63	via evaluation at `1`; the inverse is multiplication by a global scalar (`globalSMul`). The
64	substantive content is `left_inv`: every endomorphism of the unit is multiplication by its value
65	at `1` (proved from `φ`-naturality toward the terminal object of the slice). -/
66	noncomputable def unitDualSectionEquiv (X : Dᵒᵖ) :
67	    letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop
68	      (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
69	      (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
70	    (restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) ⟶
71	        restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))))
72	      ≃ₗ[(R₀.obj (op X.unop) : Type u)] (R₀.obj (op X.unop) : Type u) := by
73	  letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop
74	    (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
75	    (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
76	  exact
77	    { toFun := fun φ =>
78	        evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) X φ
79	          (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
80	      map_add' := fun φ φ' => rfl
81	      map_smul' := fun c φ => by
82	        exact DFunLike.congr_fun (evalLin_smul _ X c φ)
83	          (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
84	      invFun := fun r =>
85	        globalSMul Over.mkIdTerminal
86	          (restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) r
87	      left_inv := fun φ => by
88	        ext Y
89	        dsimp only
90	        erw [globalSMul_hom_apply]
91	        have hnat := PresheafOfModules.naturality_apply φ (Over.mkIdTerminal.from Y.unop).op
92	          (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
93	        erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one] at hnat
94	        erw [hnat, smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
95	        rfl
96	      right_inv := fun r => by
97	        change ((globalSMul Over.mkIdTerminal
98	            (restr X.unop
99	              (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) r).app
100	            (op (Over.mk (𝟙 X.unop)))).hom
101	            (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u)) = r
102	        rw [globalSMul_hom_apply, termRingMap_terminal]
103	        exact mul_one r }
104	
105	/-- **The presheaf dual of the monoidal unit is the unit**, `PresheafOfModules.dual 𝟙_ ≅ 𝟙_`,
106	assembled sectionwise from `unitDualSectionEquiv` with the evaluation-at-`1` naturality (mirroring
107	`InternalHom.internalHomEval`'s naturality at `M = 𝟙_`). -/
108	noncomputable def dualUnitIsoGen :
109	    PresheafOfModules.dual (R₀ := R₀)
110	        (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
111	      ≅ 𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :=
112	  PresheafOfModules.isoMk (fun X => (unitDualSectionEquiv X).toModuleIso)
113	    (fun {X Y} f => by
114	      refine ModuleCat.hom_ext (LinearMap.ext fun φ => ?_)
115	      change evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) Y
116	            ((PresheafOfModules.dual
117	              (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map f φ)
118	            (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj Y : Type u))
119	          = ((𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))).map f).hom
120	              (evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) X φ
121	                (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u)))
122	      have key := PresheafOfModules.naturality_apply
123	        (φ : restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) ⟶
124	          restr X.unop (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))))
125	        (Over.homMk f.unop : Over.mk f.unop ⟶ Over.mk (𝟙 X.unop)).op
126	        (1 : ((R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj X : Type u))
127	      have hrm : (restr X.unop
128	            (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map
129	          (Over.homMk f.unop : Over.mk f.unop ⟶ Over.mk (𝟙 X.unop)).op
130	          = (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))).map f := rfl
131	      rw [hrm] at key
132	      erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one] at key
133	      have hAB : (op (Over.mk (𝟙 Y.unop ≫ f.unop)) : (Over X.unop)ᵒᵖ) = op (Over.mk f.unop) :=
134	        congrArg op (congrArg Over.mk (Category.id_comp f.unop))
135	      have homAppHEq : ∀ {A B : (Over X.unop)ᵒᵖ} (_ : A = B), HEq (φ.app A) (φ.app B) := by
136	        intro A B h; subst h; rfl
137	      have hdt : evalLin (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))) Y
138	          ((PresheafOfModules.dual
139	            (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))).map f φ)
140	          = (φ.app (op (Over.mk f.unop))).hom :=
141	        congrArg ModuleCat.Hom.hom (eq_of_heq (homAppHEq hAB))
142	      exact (DFunLike.congr_fun hdt _).trans key)

177	    (((PresheafOfModules.pushforward β).obj (PresheafOfModules.dual M.val)).obj V) ≅

180	  --

184	  --   RHS carrier `Rr = (restr V.unop ((pushforward β).obj M.val) ⟶ restr V.unop 𝟙_Y)`,

190	  -- whose component at `W : (Over V.unop)ᵒᵖ` (so `W' := W.unop.left ≤ V.unop`, with image
